title: The Word and How To Read It
speaker: Mr. Isaiah Couch
series: The Word—God’s Infallible Means of Grace
source_files: 04-06-25 - The Word and How To Read It - God’s Infallible Means of Grace - Mr. Isaiah Couch.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Question Itself – WLC Q157
subpoints: The larger catechism Q157 asks: How is the Word of God to be read?
Sitting with the question before moving to the answer reveals the posture Scripture requires
A.W. Brakel: the Word is a book from which a lamb may drink and in which an elephant can drown; to reject it is spiritual murder

The catechism lists five ways the Word is to be read: with reverence, with firm persuasion, with desire to know/believe/obey, with diligence and attention, with meditation/application/self-denial/prayer

outline.title: First: With High and Reverent Esteem
subpoints: Exodus 24:7-8: Israel affirmed the covenant with the words ‘all that the Lord has said we will do and be obedient’
Nehemiah 8:3-6: when Ezra opened the book all the people stood; they answered ‘Amen, Amen’ lifting up their hands and worshiped with their faces to the ground
As children develop love for favorite books of men, how much more should we esteem the Book of the Covenant
The Bereans searched the Scriptures daily (Acts 17:11); Psalm 119: ‘I will never forget your precepts, for by them you have given me life’

outline.title: Second: With Firm Persuasion That It Is the Very Word of God
subpoints: 1 Thessalonians 2:13: the Thessalonians received it not as the word of men but as it is in truth the Word of God
1 Corinthians 2:9-12: God has revealed these things to us through his Spirit; no one knows the things of God except the Spirit of God
Brakel’s pastoral counsel for those who doubt: God humbles us to cast us entirely on him who alone grants faith; and the great cloud of witnesses in Hebrews 11 — all founded their lives on the Word
Isaiah 55:10-11: God’s Word shall accomplish what he pleases and will not return to him void

outline.title: Third: With Desire to Know, Believe, and Obey
subpoints: Ezekiel 2:9-3:3: Ezekiel eats the scroll and it was like honey and sweetness in his mouth; so we are to desire and consume God’s Word
Thomas Watson: read the Word as the last will and testament of Christ—here are your legacies of pardon, adoption, consolation, sealed in Christ’s blood
Psalm 19:10: sweeter than honey and the honeycomb
Colossians 1:9-12: that we would be filled with the knowledge of his will in all wisdom and spiritual understanding
Knowing, believing, and obeying the Word means living coram Deo—before the face of God, under his authority, to his glory

outline.title: Fourth and Fifth: With Diligence and Attention; With Meditation, Application, Self-Denial, and Prayer
subpoints: 2 Timothy 3:16: all Scripture is given by inspiration of God and profitable; it demands diligence
Calvin: the Word is the instrument by which the Lord dispenses the illumination of his Spirit; those who claim the Spirit but reject the Word are fanatics
The TLDR culture (‘too long, didn’t read’) illustrates the church’s sluggishness; the Bereans are the antidote
Joshua 1:8: this book of the law shall not depart from your mouth; meditate in it day and night
Deuteronomy 6:6-9: these words shall be in your heart; teach them diligently; bind them on your hands; write them on your doorposts
Colossians 3:16-17: let the Word of Christ dwell richly in you; whatever you do, do it in the name of Christ

summary:
Mr. Isaiah Couch delivers the eighth lesson in the Sunday school series on the Word of God as God’s infallible means of grace, focusing on the Westminster Larger Catechism Q157: “How is the Word of God to be read?” He begins by inviting the congregation to linger over the question itself before jumping to the answer, observing that the posture the question implies—reverence, dependence on the Spirit, desire to obey—is as instructive as the five-point answer.

The catechism’s answer identifies five ways the Word is to be read: with high and reverent esteem; with firm persuasion that it is the very Word of God; with desire to know, believe, and obey; with diligence and attention to its matter and scope; and with meditation, application, self-denial, and prayer. Isaiah Couch works through each of these in turn, grounding each point in Scripture and drawing heavily on the 17th-century Dutch theologian Wilhelmus a Brakel, whose systematic theology provides both the doctrinal structure and the pastoral warmth of the lesson.

Key biblical illustrations include the covenant ratification in Exodus 24 (Israel’s reverent posture before the Word), the six-hour public reading in Nehemiah 8, Ezekiel eating the scroll (the Word as honey and sweetness), Thomas Watson’s imagery of the Bible as Christ’s last will and testament, and the Bereans of Acts 17 as the model of diligence. Calvin is quoted on the inseparability of the Word and the Spirit. The lesson concludes with the comprehensive vision of Deuteronomy 6 and Colossians 3:16-17—the Word is to be ever before us and ever within us: taught to our children, bound on our hands, written on our doorposts, dwelling richly in our hearts.
